slide.title: The Execution Path
bullets: One query flows top-down: Driver → Catalyst → Tungsten → DAG → executors
Geometry is the lesson: stacked = sequential, side-by-side = parallel
Each stage hands off to the next; tasks within a stage run in parallel

narration.text: Every Spark query takes the same journey, from the driver down to the executors that do the work.

slide.title: The Driver
bullets: Runs your application code
Creates the SparkSession and the logical plan
Coordinates the whole job

slide.title: Catalyst Optimizer
bullets: Unresolved → Logical → Optimized → Physical plan
Rule-based and cost-based optimization
Produces the most efficient physical plan to run

slide.title: DAG Scheduler
bullets: Splits the job into stages at shuffle boundaries
Stages run in sequence; each waits for the previous shuffle
Each stage is a set of tasks — one per partition

slide.title: Cluster Manager
bullets: Allocates executors on the cluster
Pluggable backend: Local, Standalone, YARN, Kubernetes, Databricks
Independent of the rest of the execution path

slide.title: Executors
bullets: Run the dispatched tasks in parallel
Hold data partitions in memory
Report results back to the driver

slide.title: RDDs — the Bedrock
bullets: Every DataFrame and SQL query compiles down to RDDs
The shape of this scene is the lesson: create → transform → act
Transforms split into narrow (no shuffle), wide (shuffle), and pair (key/value)

narration.text: Before DataFrames existed, there were RDDs. Everything in Spark still runs on top of them, so understanding the RDD model explains how the whole engine behaves.

slide.title: What an RDD Actually Is
bullets: Resilient Distributed Dataset — an immutable, partitioned collection
Resilient: rebuildable from lineage; Distributed: split across executors
You describe transformations; nothing runs until an action fires

narration.text: An RDD is a resilient distributed dataset. Resilient because Spark can rebuild any lost piece from its lineage. Distributed because it is split into partitions spread across the cluster. And immutable, so every transformation produces a brand new RDD.

slide.title: Partitions — the Unit of Parallelism
bullets: An RDD is a set of partitions; one task processes one partition
More partitions means more parallelism — up to your core count
numSlices on parallelize, or the source layout, sets the count

narration.text: A partition is the unit of parallelism. Spark runs one task per partition, so the number of partitions sets how much work can happen at once. Too few and you leave cores idle; too many and the scheduling overhead dominates.

slide.title: Lineage — Fault Recovery
bullets: Each RDD remembers the chain of transformations that built it
Lose a partition and Spark recomputes only that slice from its parents
This lineage graph is why RDDs are 'resilient' — no replication needed

narration.text: Spark does not replicate data to survive failures. Instead each RDD records its lineage, the recipe of transformations that produced it. If an executor dies, Spark replays just the missing partition's recipe. That is the resilient in resilient distributed dataset.

slide.title: SparkContext — the Entry Point
bullets: RDDs come from the SparkContext, reachable as spark.sparkContext
It is the low-level handle the SparkSession wraps
All RDD creation methods hang off this object

narration.text: Where DataFrames start from the SparkSession, RDDs start from the SparkContext. You can reach it through spark dot sparkContext. It is the older, lower-level entry point that the session quietly wraps.

slide.title: Creating RDDs
bullets: parallelize — distribute an in-memory collection (great for demos)
textFile — one record per line; wholeTextFiles — one record per file
numSlices controls how many partitions parallelize produces

narration.text: There are two ways to get an RDD. Parallelize takes a collection that already lives in the driver and spreads it across the cluster. Text file and whole text files read from storage — line by line, or one whole file per record.

slide.title: Transformations vs Actions
bullets: Transformations are lazy — they only build the lineage graph
Actions trigger execution and return a value to the driver or write out
If your job seems instant, you have only declared transformations

narration.text: Two kinds of operations. Transformations like map and filter are lazy; they just extend the recipe. Actions like collect and count are what actually run the job and hand a result back. Nothing computes until an action asks for it.

slide.title: RDDs of Objects — an OOP Note
bullets: An RDD can hold any object — Spark sees an opaque function over elements
Contrast: a DataFrame sees columns and can optimize them
Power and flexibility, but Catalyst can't help you here

narration.text: An RDD can hold any object you like, and your transformation is just an arbitrary function. That is flexible, but it also means Spark cannot see inside it. With a DataFrame, Spark understands the columns and can optimize; with an RDD it runs your code blind.

slide.title: Narrow vs Wide Transformations
bullets: Narrow: each output partition depends on one input partition — no shuffle
Wide: output partitions pull from many inputs — forces a shuffle
Shuffles are the expensive part; wide transforms mark stage boundaries

narration.text: The single most important distinction. A narrow transformation, like map or filter, keeps each partition independent — no data crosses the network. A wide transformation, like groupByKey or join, must reshuffle data across partitions. Shuffles are slow, so knowing which is which tells you where the cost lives.

slide.title: Pair RDDs — the (key, value) View
bullets: An RDD of tuples unlocks the byKey family of operations
mapToPair / keyBy turn elements into (key, value) pairs
Keys decide how data is grouped and shuffled

narration.text: When an RDD holds key value pairs, a whole new family of operations opens up: the byKey methods. The key controls how Spark groups and shuffles your data, which makes pair RDDs the foundation of aggregation.

slide.title: reduceByKey vs groupByKey
bullets: reduceByKey combines values map-side first — less data shuffled
groupByKey ships every value across the network, then groups
The exam classic: prefer reduceByKey for associative aggregations

narration.text: A favourite exam question. Both can sum values per key, but reduceByKey combines partially on each partition before the shuffle, so far less data crosses the network. GroupByKey ships everything first. Reach for reduceByKey whenever your operation is associative.

slide.title: Persistence — cache & persist
bullets: By default an RDD recomputes from lineage on every action
cache() keeps it in memory; persist(level) picks the StorageLevel
Cache only when you reuse an RDD — caching once-used data wastes memory

narration.text: Because RDDs are lazy and lineage-based, reusing one recomputes it from scratch each time. Cache and persist break that cycle by storing the materialized partitions. Persist lets you choose the storage level — memory, disk, or a mix. Only cache data you will actually reuse.

slide.title: Partition-Level Operations
bullets: mapPartitions runs your function once per partition, not per element
repartition reshuffles to any count; coalesce only shrinks, no shuffle
Use coalesce to reduce partitions cheaply before writing

narration.text: Sometimes you work a whole partition at a time. Map partitions runs once per partition — handy for setting up a connection once instead of per row. Repartition reshuffles to any number of partitions; coalesce only shrinks, and avoids a full shuffle, which makes it the cheap way to consolidate before a write.

slide.title: Broadcast & Accumulators
bullets: Broadcast: ship a read-only copy of a value to every executor once
Accumulator: a write-only counter the driver aggregates across tasks
Both avoid the trap of capturing large objects in a closure

narration.text: Two shared variables solve two problems. A broadcast variable sends one read-only copy of a lookup table to each executor, instead of bundling it with every task. An accumulator is a counter that tasks add to and the driver reads back, useful for metrics.

slide.title: RDD vs DataFrame — When to Reach for Each
bullets: Default to DataFrames: Catalyst and Tungsten optimize them for you
Drop to RDDs for unstructured data or fine-grained control
Mixing is fine — convert with .rdd and back via toDF / createDataFrame

narration.text: So when do you actually use raw RDDs? Almost always, prefer DataFrames — the optimizer makes them faster with less effort. Reach for RDDs only when your data has no structure, or you need precise control the higher-level API cannot give. And you can convert between the two whenever you need.

slide.title: One Engine, Four APIs
bullets: The vertical spine is the dataflow: Input → Read → process → Write → Output
RDD, DataFrame, SQL and pandas are interchangeable lenses over the same engine
Cross-cutting concerns (cache, shuffle, tuning) apply to every API

narration.text: Spark gives you four ways to express the same job. Pick the lens that fits the problem.

slide.title: Reading Data — spark.read
bullets: One reader for many sources: CSV, Parquet, JSON, JDBC, Delta
Control schema, format options and bad-record handling
Read multiple files / partitions as one DataFrame

slide.title: RDD API — the low-level core
bullets: create → transform → act
Narrow vs wide transformations decide where shuffles happen
Pair RDDs power key-based operations like reduceByKey

slide.title: DataFrame API — the workhorse
bullets: Typed columns with built-in functions, joins and aggregations
Catalyst optimizes the plan for you
The default choice for most batch work

slide.title: SQL API — Spark in plain SQL
bullets: Register a view, then SELECT against it
Joins, aggregations and functions in standard SQL
Same engine and optimizer as the DataFrame API

slide.title: pandas API on Spark
bullets: Write familiar pandas code, run it distributed
Bridge for analysts moving from single-node pandas
Backed by Spark's distributed execution

slide.title: Writing Data — df.write
bullets: Choose format, save mode and output layout (partitionBy)
Write to files, Hive, JDBC or Delta
Layout choices here shape how the data reads back

slide.title: Batch in a Loop
bullets: A stream is a series of micro-batches over an unbounded table
Offsets track progress; triggers pace each batch
State + checkpoint give exactly-once and fault recovery

narration.text: Structured streaming is just the batch engine, run over and over on small slices of an endless table.

slide.title: Event-time Windows
bullets: Bucket events by event time, not arrival time
Tumbling, sliding and session windows
Aggregations run per window

slide.title: Watermarking
bullets: The watermark line splits accepted from dropped late data
Bounds how long state is kept for a window
Trades a little completeness for bounded memory

slide.title: Caching & the Memory Model
bullets: cache / persist keep a reused DataFrame in memory
Storage levels trade memory for CPU and disk
Cache only what you reuse — it isn't free

slide.title: Taming the Shuffle
bullets: The shuffle is usually the bottleneck
Tune partitioning and shuffle config to match data size
Broadcast small tables to avoid shuffling the big one
